info.text: In this example I'm going to show you how to make HTTP requests to REST APIs in order to access resources on other servers. There are old (and even older) ways of writing code that makes HTTP requests, but this is the most modern.
info.title: getting started
asynchronicity
info.text: JavaScript code typically runs "synchronously", meaning one line at a time, each line waiting for the line before it to finish executing before the next line runs. But there are often situations where we need to write "asynchronous code", where parts of our code wait for something to happen before getting called. This sort of code can get messy and often leads to "callback hell"

info.title: async / await
info.text: In this example we're using newer JavaScript syntax known as async functions which allows us to write asynchronous code in a synchronous fashion, this generally makes our code both easier to write and easier to read.

info.title: async setup
info.text: In order to create an "async function", we need to include the async keyword before our function definition. Async functions allows us to write JavaScript "Promises" (a form of asynchronous code explained in another Fetch demo) as though they were synchronous by including the await keyword before we call the promise function.

info.title: fetch
info.text: Here I'm using the dog.ceo REST API to request a random dog image. When using a third party REST API like this it's important to check out their documentation page, that's where you'll find what other URL endpoints we can send requests to as well as what type of data we should expect in return.

info.title: other APIs
info.text: There are loads of REST APIs on the Web for us to experiment with, you can explore some listed on sites like API List and Public APIs. Keep in mind that some APIs require you sign up for a "key", in these instances there may be a limit to how many times you can request data for free before before you need to pay.

info.title: response
info.text: Because I'm using the await keyword (which can only be used inside an async functions) the Response object gets returned by the Fetch API and stored in the res variable. The await keyword also guarantees that the next line (which makes use of the res object) won't fire until the REST API has sent us its response.

info.title: JSON data
info.text: The Response object's json() method (which is used to parse the response data as a JSON object) is another instance of a JavaScript Promise (asynchronous function), which means we can use the await keyword again to wait for it to return the parsed object before storing it into our json variable.

info.title: API docs
info.text: It's important to note, while JSON is the most common response type, not all REST APIs return data this way, it's important to check their documentation to know for sure. In this case the documentation for the Dog CEO API confirms that this endpoint returns JSON.

info.title: log data
info.text: Once the data from our response has been parsed into a json object it's always a good idea to log that object to the console to make sure the data we got back is what we were expecting. You can open your browser's console by pressing F12 (or Fn+F12 on Mac). This is also great for catching errors.

info.title: nn.min.js
info.text: In this example, our JSON object, which we've called data, has a message property which contains a URL string to a random dog image which we can use to to set as the src of a new <img> element. To make it a little easier to create new HTML elements I've imported the netnet standard library at the top of our page.

info.title: create img
info.text: With the nn library we can .create() a new img element, .set() it's "src" attribute to our new random dog image url, stored in data.message of our response. These images come in different sizes so we'll use the .css() method to make sure they all conform to the same width, and then we'll use .addTo() to add our new dog image to the body of our page.

info.title: oad and clickl
info.text: As with many of our other demos, the last line in our code sets everything going by running our setup function as soon as our page has loaded. I've also added a call to that function on our window's "click" event.

info.title: try it
info.text: Try clicking the page a few time to send new requests to the Dog CEO API, we'll get a new random dog image each time.
